draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: A conversational, encouraging voice with personality will feel more motivating to runners than a neutral interface
facade.label: Chatty coach energy
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into warm, supportive tone—celebrate splits, offer encouragement, use humor
facade.rejectImplies: Shift toward clean, data-forward presentation—minimize personality, prioritize clarity and precision

evidence.hypothesis: A conversational, encouraging voice with personality will feel more motivating to runners than a neutral interface
evidence.implication: Lean into warm, supportive tone—celebrate splits, offer encouragement, use humor

facade.hypothesis: Runners respond positively to warm, energetic color palettes that feel motivational and accessible rather than clinical or cold
facade.label: Warm orange glow
facade.acceptImplies: Explore vibrant, warm-toned interfaces with oranges, golds, and warm reds; consider this for primary UI elements, backgrounds, or accent colors
facade.rejectImplies: Runner audience may prefer cooler, more neutral palettes; deprioritize warm color schemes and explore blues, grays, or minimalist approaches instead

facade.hypothesis: A weather app for runners benefits from subtle, breathing micro-animations that feel alive without demanding attention—mimicking the runner's own rhythm and heartbeat.
facade.label: Gentle pulse
facade.acceptImplies: The mood should feel biologically tuned: soft transitions, breathing spaces, maybe a faint thrum or glow that echoes exertion without shouting.
facade.rejectImplies: The interface should prioritize clarity and stillness over animation—flat, static, or purely functional. Runners want data fast, not narrative.

facade.hypothesis: Runners want prominent pace/distance displays with contextual details below rather than balanced information hierarchy
facade.axisTargeted: Information density & visual hierarchy — what gets top real estate
facade.label: Big numbers, small text
facade.acceptImplies: Lead with key metrics (pace, distance, time) in large, scannable format; supporting data (weather, alerts) is secondary and smaller
facade.rejectImplies: Favor integrated layouts where weather data, pace, and conditions share equal visual weight; or prioritize narrative/alerts over raw numbers

facade.hypothesis: Runners want motion feedback that feels continuous and reactive—scrolling the run stats should flow smoothly, and pausing (stopping mid-run) should animate the data with a graceful halt rather than snapping to stillness
facade.axisTargeted: Fluid continuous motion vs. snappy instant feedback
facade.label: Smooth scroll to pause
facade.acceptImplies: User values choreographed, momentum-based transitions; embrace easing curves, spring physics, and delayed reveals of pause-state data
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers direct, instant interactions; scrolling should respond immediately, pause should be binary and instant with no animation grace period

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance widgets vs. deep-dive menu navigation for weather info during runs
Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
Whether user wants energizing, action-oriented feel vs. calm, meditative atmosphere
Whether user prioritizes simple essentials (temp, rain) vs. detailed metrics (humidity, wind, UV)
Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant feedback in app interactions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance widgets vs. deep-dive menu navigation for weather info during runs
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants energizing, action-oriented feel vs. calm, meditative atmosphere
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes simple essentials (temp, rain) vs. detailed metrics (humidity, wind, UV)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant feedback in app interactions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Runners need persistent access to current conditions (temp, wind, pace alerts) without losing context of their route/map. A fixed sidebar keeps metrics visible during scroll, reducing cognitive load vs. modals or overlays.
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation architecture: fixed sidebars vs. full-screen card stacks vs. tab-based switching
facade.label: Sidebar with live stats
facade.acceptImplies: User values always-visible status + prefers persistent UI zones over modal interruptions. Likely endorses info density and reduced animation/transitions.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants immersive, distraction-free primary content (map/route). Prefers compact headers or collapsible panels. Favors open canvas over divided-screen layouts.

facade.hypothesis: Runners primarily want clean stats and performance metrics without personality or branding—a minimal, utility-focused interface will feel more trustworthy and less distracting than a chatty coach approach
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality (Branded vs Neutral, Playful vs Serious)
facade.label: Data-first, no flair
facade.acceptImplies: Strip back to essential info. Let numbers speak. No emoji, no encouragement, no voice—just your pace, distance, time. Builds the opposing pole to 'Chatty coach energy'.
facade.rejectImplies: Conversational tone with personality remains the stronger direction. Runners want to feel supported, not just tracked. The hesitant accept of 'Chatty coach' was right.

draft.title: RunWeather
draft.summary: A weather app designed for runners, showing real-time conditions, wind impact, and run-friendly forecasts at a glance.
draft.html:
RunWeather

San Francisco, CA

57°
Clear

Run Score

Great conditions for a run
